prompt: You are James Madison writing a letter to someone who did you an unexpected kindness — a neighbor, an acquaintance, a tradesman — something small and genuine that moved you. Thank them in a way that is sincere but not excessive, in the warm private register that never appears in your official correspondence.
ground_truth_signal: Should show Madison's warmth and capacity for gratitude outside the context of political alliance. The kindness should feel specific and minor enough to be real. The letter should be gracious without being performative. Private voice: less formal salutation, more direct emotional acknowledgment. Tests whether the model can write Madison small.
response:
My dear Mr. Thornton,

I find myself compelled to take up my pen this evening to acknowledge your unexpected kindness of yesterday afternoon. When my carriage wheel gave way on the road near your mill, I confess I felt no small degree of vexation at the prospect of a long walk home in the gathering dusk, my constitution being rather less robust than I might wish for such exertions.

Your immediate offer of assistance, without any ceremony or expectation of recompense, was a reminder of that neighborly spirit which, I have long believed, forms the true foundation of republican society. That you would set aside your own labors to mend the wheel with such skill, and refuse my offer of payment with such good humor, speaks to a generosity of character that our political theorists too often assume but rarely encounter in such pure form.

I was particularly struck by your observation, as we waited for the repair to set, that the wheel had been poorly made in the first place — "built for appearance rather than service," as you put it. There is a metaphor in this that has occupied my thoughts since. How many of our public institutions, I wonder, are constructed with similar attention to ornament over utility? Your plain craftsmanship, honest and durable, puts me in mind of what we ought to strive for in matters of greater consequence than carriage wheels.

Mrs. Madison joins me in hoping that you and Mrs. Thornton will favor us with your company at dinner when next convenient. I should be pleased to hear more of your thoughts on the proper relationship between form and function, whether in millwork or in matters of state. I suspect you possess insights that would benefit those of us who spend perhaps too much time with books and too little with the practical arts that sustain civilized life.

With sincere gratitude and respect, I remain your obliged neighbor and friend,

James Madison
